Name: LatencyCriticalPACBayesInference
Title: Latency-Critical PAC-Bayes Inference for Efficient Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks, we can develop a latency-aware inference protocol that ensures strict wall-clock budgets while maintaining high accuracy. This approach will be particularly valuable for real-time systems where latency is critical.
Related Work: Existing work in PAC-Bayes frameworks has primarily focused on theoretical bounds for generalization and robustness (McAllester, 1999; Langford and Seeger, 2001). Recent studies have explored latency-aware inference but have not integrated PAC-Bayes bounds (Chen et al., 2020). Our proposal uniquely combines these two areas to address the specific needs of latency-critical applications.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to latency-critical inference using PAC-Bayes frameworks. By deriving strictly bounded-overhead protocols, we aim to ensure that inference times meet strict wall-clock budgets while maintaining high accuracy. Our method leverages Jacobian-based layer-skipping to minimize hidden-state perturbation and uses uncertainty-quantified activation proxies for online routing. We will empirically validate our approach using latency benchmarks and evaluate its performance on throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers. This work is crucial for real-time systems where latency is a critical constraint.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Develop a PAC-Bayes framework for latency-critical inference. Derive the theoretical bounds for wall-clock budgets and accuracy.
Experiment 2: Implement the Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism to minimize hidden-state perturbation. Evaluate its impact on latency and accuracy.
Experiment 3: Use uncertainty-quantified activation proxies for online routing. Compare the performance with traditional routing methods.
Experiment 4: Conduct latency benchmarks to evaluate the throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers of our approach. Use datasets such as ImageNet and CIFAR-10 for comprehensive testing.
Experiment 5: Perform structured perturbation and domain shift evaluations to assess the robustness of our method. Quantify the stability radii under different conditions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical derivation of PAC-Bayes bounds for latency-critical inference may be complex and require significant mathematical rigor.
The implementation of Jacobian-based layer-skipping may introduce additional computational overhead, which needs to be carefully managed to meet latency constraints.
The use of uncertainty-quantified activation proxies may introduce variance in the routing decisions, which could affect the overall performance.
The latency benchmarks may not fully capture all real-world scenarios, and additional testing may be required to validate the approach in practical settings.
